title: Flystrike prevention in sheep
subtitle: Identifying high-risk areas, timing preventive treatment, and acting fast if strike takes hold.
excerpt: Flystrike kills sheep quickly. Blowflies lay eggs in damp, soiled, or wounded wool, and the hatched maggots can kill a sheep within 24 hours. This tutorial covers the risk windows, the body areas to check, how to time preventive treatments, and what to do when you find a struck sheep.
sourceNotes:
AHDB Beef and Lamb, Better Returns Programme: Sheep Health and Welfare (2013). https://ahdb.org.uk
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Sheep (1998). https://www.gov.uk
NADAIS flystrike guidance for small flock keepers. https://www.nadis.org.uk

glossaryTerms.definition: A condition caused by blowfly larvae (maggots) feeding on live sheep tissue. The green-bottle fly Lucilia sericata is the main culprit in the UK. Flies are attracted to damp, soiled, or broken skin; maggots hatch within hours and burrow into flesh, releasing toxins that can kill an affected sheep within 24 to 48 hours.

Clumps of dried or semi-dry faeces matted into the wool around the tail and breech area. Dags keep the skin moist and attract blowflies, making them the single highest-risk site for flystrike in UK sheep.

An insecticide applied along the sheep's back to prevent blowfly strike. Common active ingredients are dicyclanil and cypermethrin. Each product carries a licensed protection period; applying too early or too late leaves the flock exposed during peak fly season.

content.text: Blowflies are active whenever temperatures exceed 10 degrees Celsius. In the UK that means risk from April through October, with the peak in July and August. Check your flock at least twice a day during warm, humid weather. Any sheep that is off feed, stamping, or biting at its fleece needs examining immediately.
The three highest-risk sites
The breech and tail area is the primary risk zone.
Dags

keep the skin moist and warm. Any sheep with loose or dirty wool around the tail needs that wool clipped off before preventive treatment is applied. The second risk zone is the area around any wound, including foot rot lesions, castration sites, and skin tears from fencing. Flies locate wounds by smell and can strike within minutes of the skin breaking. The third zone is the neck and shoulder wool on recently shorn sheep: moisture trapped against the skin from heavy dew or rain creates suitable conditions for a few days after shearing.
Timing preventive treatment
Apply a
preventive pour-on

before fly season starts rather than after your first case. Check the product data sheet for the licensed protection window. Dicyclanil products protect for 8 to 16 weeks depending on dose; cypermethrin products protect for around 6 weeks. Apply at shearing or in late April to early May, before flies reach their peak. Do not apply to wet fleece: absorption drops and protection is unreliable. Record the treatment date and the product used in your flock health record.

attrs.title: Withdrawal periods apply
attrs.body: Most flystrike preventives carry a meat withdrawal period of 35 to 100 days. Check the data sheet before treating any sheep close to slaughter and record the treatment date against individual ear tag numbers.

content.text: Recognising early strike
Early
flystrike

is easy to miss at a distance. Look for any sheep that is separating itself from the group, holding its head low, or stamping its back feet. Move the animal to a pen and part the fleece around the breech, tail base, and any wound sites. Early strike looks like yellowed or discoloured wool with a faint smell. Parting the wool reveals the maggots, which are cream-white and 3 to 10 mm long depending on how long they have been feeding.
What to do if you find a struck sheep
Bring the sheep in immediately. Do not leave a struck animal in the field overnight.
Clip all affected wool away with sharp dagging shears. Remove it from the area.
Remove every maggot you can see by hand or with a stiff brush. They cluster at the wound edges and burrow inward.
Apply a licensed wound treatment to kill remaining maggots and deter re-strike.

Call your vet if the wound is large, deep, or the sheep is showing signs of systemic illness: high temperature, off feed, or reluctance to rise. Struck sheep deteriorate fast and may need antibiotics and pain relief.
Reducing ongoing risk

Dag sheep regularly throughout summer. Any sheep prone to scouring should be checked every other day during warm weather. Worm burdens cause loose dung; bringing worm burdens down reduces dag formation and cuts strike risk at the same time. Shear on time: the longer the fleece, the longer any moisture stays trapped against the skin.
